content.text: Collaboration is how agents in a crew share work, information, and results. CrewAI supports four principal collaboration patterns: sequential hand-off, hub-and-spoke delegation, peer review, and parallel fan-out. The right pattern depends on whether the workflow is linear, open-ended, quality-sensitive, or throughput-sensitive.
The simplest collaboration model: tasks run in list order, and each task can receive the prior task's output via
. Agent A produces, agent B refines, agent C publishes.

from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew, Process

researcher = Agent(role="Researcher", goal="Gather data on {topic}", backstory="...")
analyst    = Agent(role="Analyst",    goal="Interpret gathered data",  backstory="...")
writer     = Agent(role="Writer",     goal="Write the final report",   backstory="...")

gather   = Task(description="Research {topic}", expected_output="Raw notes and sources", agent=researcher)
analyse  = Task(description="Analyse the research and identify trends",
                expected_output="Structured analysis with key insights",
                agent=analyst, context=[gather])
report   = Task(description="Write a 600-word executive report",
                expected_output="Polished report in markdown",
                agent=writer, context=[analyse])

crew = Crew(agents=[researcher, analyst, writer], tasks=[gather, analyse, report],
            process=Process.sequential, verbose=True)
result = crew.kickoff(inputs={"topic": "AI adoption in healthcare"})
Design rule: list tasks in execution order and wire
for every upstream dependency. Missing context is the most common cause of agents producing outputs that ignore prior work.
receives a broad task and delegates sub-tasks to domain specialists. The coordinator synthesises results and produces the final deliverable. Specialists have

coordinator = Agent(
    role="Research Coordinator",
    goal="Produce a multi-domain report by orchestrating specialists",
    backstory=(
        "You never write content directly. You delegate every research sub-task "
        "to the domain expert and synthesise their reports into one coherent document."
    ),
    allow_delegation=True,
    verbose=True,
)
climate = Agent(role="Climate Scientist",  goal="...", backstory="...", allow_delegation=False)
econ    = Agent(role="Economist",          goal="...", backstory="...", allow_delegation=False)
policy  = Agent(role="Policy Analyst",     goal="...", backstory="...", allow_delegation=False)

big_task = Task(
    description=(
        "Produce a 10-page report on climate change policy for G7 governments. "
        "Cover: scientific consensus, economic impact, and specific policy recommendations."
    ),
    expected_output="10-page structured report",
    agent=coordinator,
)

crew = Crew(agents=[coordinator, climate, econ, policy], tasks=[big_task], verbose=True)
result = crew.kickoff()
The coordinator's backstory is the critical lever — instruct it explicitly to delegate rather than write content itself. Without that instruction, the LLM may attempt everything directly.
Quality-sensitive workflows benefit from a review chain: an author produces a draft, a reviewer checks it, and an editor approves or refines it. Each agent in the chain sees all prior outputs via

author   = Agent(role="Author",   goal="Write a first draft", backstory="...")
reviewer = Agent(role="Reviewer", goal="Check factual accuracy and structure", backstory="...")
editor   = Agent(role="Editor",   goal="Polish language and ensure publication readiness", backstory="...")

draft_task = Task(
    description="Write a 500-word draft on {topic}",
    expected_output="A 500-word draft",
    agent=author,
)
review_task = Task(
    description=(
        "Review the draft for factual accuracy and logical structure. "
        "Output 'APPROVED' if it passes, or 'REJECTED: <issues>' listing specific problems."
    ),
    expected_output="APPROVED or REJECTED with issues",
    agent=reviewer,
    context=[draft_task],
)
edit_task = Task(
    description=(
        "If the review is APPROVED, polish the draft for publication. "
        "If REJECTED, revise it to address every listed issue, then deliver the final version."
    ),
    expected_output="Publication-ready article",
    agent=editor,
    context=[draft_task, review_task],
)

crew = Crew(agents=[author, reviewer, editor], tasks=[draft_task, review_task, edit_task],
            process=Process.sequential)
When independent sub-tasks can run simultaneously — multiple language translations, parallel domain research, independent code reviews — use

from crewai import Crew, Process
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

translator_en = Agent(role="English Translator",  goal="...", backstory="...", allow_delegation=False)
translator_de = Agent(role="German Translator",   goal="...", backstory="...", allow_delegation=False)
translator_fr = Agent(role="French Translator",   goal="...", backstory="...", allow_delegation=False)

translate_en = Task(description="Translate {text} to English",  expected_output="English translation",  agent=translator_en)
translate_de = Task(description="Translate {text} to German",   expected_output="German translation",   agent=translator_de)
translate_fr = Task(description="Translate {text} to French",   expected_output="French translation",   agent=translator_fr)

crew = Crew(
    agents=[translator_en, translator_de, translator_fr],
    tasks=[translate_en, translate_de, translate_fr],
    process=Process.hierarchical,
    manager_llm=ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini"),
    verbose=True,
)
result = crew.kickoff(inputs={"text": "The product launches next quarter."})
Three mechanisms move data between agents — choose by use case:
Known upstream dependency in sequential process
Full text of listed task outputs
Ad-hoc sub-task at runtime via DelegateWorkTool
Text explicitly passed by delegating agent
Cross-run recall, entity tracking, semantic retrieval

# Pattern: combine context + memory for maximum information flow
crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, analyst, writer],
    tasks=[
        Task(description="Research {topic}", expected_output="...", agent=researcher),
        Task(description="Analyse", expected_output="...", agent=analyst,
             context=[tasks[0]]),           # explicit upstream data
        Task(description="Write report", expected_output="...", agent=writer,
             context=[tasks[0], tasks[1]]), # both upstream tasks
    ],
    memory=True,    # also enables long-term + entity recall from previous runs
)
Multiple agents can share the same tool instance. CrewAI's tool caching (
default) ensures the same call is not re-executed — important when agents share a slow or paid API tool.

from crewai_tools import SerperDevTool

shared_search = SerperDevTool()  # one instance, shared across agents

researcher = Agent(role="Researcher", ..., tools=[shared_search])
fact_checker = Agent(role="Fact Checker", ..., tools=[shared_search])

# If both agents call search("quantum computing breakthroughs 2024"):
# - researcher runs it first, result is cached
# - fact_checker gets the cached result instantly at no extra API cost
In large crews where multiple agents collaborate, wire
to build a live audit trail of which agent completed what.

completed_tasks = []

def on_task_done(task_output):
    completed_tasks.append({
        "agent": task_output.agent,
        "summary": task_output.summary,
        "chars": len(task_output.raw),
    })
    print(f"[{task_output.agent}] completed ({len(task_output.raw)} chars)")

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, analyst, writer],
    tasks=[gather, analyse, report],
    task_callback=on_task_done,
)
result = crew.kickoff()

print("
Collaboration audit trail:")
for entry in completed_tasks:
    print(f"  {entry['agent']}: {entry['summary']}")
: one agent assigned all tasks with
. Result: the LLM tries to do everything itself rather than genuinely collaborating. Decompose into focused agents with single responsibilities.
: agent B uses the output of agent A but
is not declared. The LLM guesses what A produced, introducing hallucinations. Always declare dependencies explicitly.
: agent A delegates to B, B delegates back to A. This creates an infinite loop. Set
Too many agents for simple tasks
: three agents for a task that requires one adds latency and token cost with no quality gain. Use a single agent for straightforward generation tasks.
: in a crew, backstories serve as implicit routing instructions. If the backstory does not tell the agent
vs. work alone, the LLM defaults to doing everything itself.
(known step order, no parallelism) → sequential hand-off.
(scope not known at design time) → hub-and-spoke with coordinator.
(must pass review before publishing) → peer review chain.
(multiple domains, languages, or components) → parallel fan-out with hierarchical process.
When a task produces structured output (Pydantic model or JSON), downstream agents receive it as text in their
. Include a clear schema explanation in the downstream task description so the LLM knows how to parse it.

from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import List

class ResearchFindings(BaseModel):
    topic: str
    key_points: List[str]
    confidence: float

research_task = Task(
    description="Research {topic} and return structured findings",
    expected_output="JSON matching ResearchFindings schema",
    agent=researcher,
    output_pydantic=ResearchFindings,
)

# Downstream agent sees: {"topic": "...", "key_points": [...], "confidence": 0.9}
analysis_task = Task(
    description=(
        "You will receive a JSON object with keys: topic, key_points (list), confidence (float). "
        "Write a 300-word analysis of the key points, noting the confidence level."
    ),
    expected_output="300-word analysis",
    agent=analyst,
    context=[research_task],
)
All agents in a crew receive the same
dict at kickoff. This is the idiomatic way to pass configuration (topic, target audience, deadline, style guide) to every agent without duplicating it in each task description.

researcher = Agent(
    role="Researcher",
    goal="Research {topic} for a {audience} audience",
    backstory="...",
)
writer = Agent(
    role="Writer",
    goal="Write in a {style} style for {audience}",
    backstory="...",
)

research_task = Task(
    description="Research the latest developments in {topic}",
    expected_output="Comprehensive summary suitable for {audience}",
    agent=researcher,
)
write_task = Task(
    description="Write a {style} article about {topic} for {audience}",
    expected_output="Ready-to-publish article",
    agent=writer,
    context=[research_task],
)

crew = Crew(agents=[researcher, writer], tasks=[research_task, write_task])
result = crew.kickoff(inputs={
    "topic": "quantum computing",
    "audience": "C-suite executives",
    "style": "concise and jargon-free",
})
For very large workflows, split work across multiple crews connected by Python code. The output of
. This is not a native CrewAI feature — it is plain Python orchestration.

# Stage 1: Research crew
research_crew = Crew(agents=[researcher, fact_checker], tasks=[gather, verify])
research_result = research_crew.kickoff(inputs={"topic": "fusion energy"})

# Stage 2: Publishing crew receives the research output
publish_crew = Crew(agents=[writer, editor, publisher], tasks=[draft, review, publish])
final_result = publish_crew.kickoff(inputs={
    "research": research_result.raw,
    "topic": "fusion energy",
})

Multi-crew pipelines increase modularity and let each crew be tested independently. The trade-off is that cross-crew memory and entity tracking do not work out of the box — each crew has its own memory store.
Every agent-to-agent hand-off adds tokens: the receiving agent's prompt includes the prior task's full output. Estimate the per-run token cost before scaling:

# After a crew.kickoff(), inspect token usage
result = crew.kickoff()
usage = result.token_usage
print(f"Total tokens: {usage.total_tokens}")
print(f"  Prompt tokens: {usage.prompt_tokens}")
print(f"  Completion tokens: {usage.completion_tokens}")

# If total_tokens is too high:
# 1. Summarise task outputs before passing them as context
# 2. Use a cheaper/smaller model for intermediate agents
# 3. Reduce the number of items in context=[] to the minimum needed
For cost-sensitive pipelines, assign your capable (and expensive) model only to the agents that need deep reasoning, and use a smaller model (e.g.
Integration tests for multi-agent collaboration are slow and expensive. Use these strategies to keep the feedback loop fast:
: replace the agent's LLM with a deterministic mock that returns canned responses. CrewAI uses LangChain LLMs under the hood — use
Test one task at a time
in isolation before wiring tasks into a crew.
Use short, cheap prompts in CI
: parameterise the task description to use a 50-word output in test environments, reserving 500-word outputs for production.
: check that context was received (output contains keywords from prior task), not that the exact wording matches — LLM output is non-deterministic.

import pytest
from langchain_community.chat_models.fake import FakeListChatModel

def test_sequential_collaboration():
    # Mock LLM returns canned responses
    fake_llm = FakeListChatModel(responses=[
        "Research findings: quantum supremacy achieved in 2023.",
        "Analysis: a 23% performance gain over classical algorithms.",
        "Report: Quantum computing showed a 23% gain per research findings.",
    ])
    researcher = Agent(role="Researcher", goal="...", backstory="...", llm=fake_llm)
    analyst    = Agent(role="Analyst",    goal="...", backstory="...", llm=fake_llm)
    writer     = Agent(role="Writer",     goal="...", backstory="...", llm=fake_llm)

    gather  = Task(description="Research quantum computing", expected_output="...", agent=researcher)
    analyse = Task(description="Analyse",  expected_output="...", agent=analyst, context=[gather])
    report  = Task(description="Write",    expected_output="...", agent=writer,  context=[analyse])

    crew = Crew(agents=[researcher, analyst, writer], tasks=[gather, analyse, report])
    result = crew.kickoff()

    # Structural assertion: final output references analyst finding
    assert "23%" in result.raw
wires outputs forward — best for deterministic linear pipelines.
orchestrates specialists — best for open-ended multi-domain tasks.
: author → reviewer → editor, each receiving prior context — best for quality enforcement.
with manager LLM dispatches concurrent sub-tasks — best for throughput and independent work.
for maximum information flow across the collaboration.
to build an audit trail of which agent produced what in complex multi-agent crews.
